strands.title: Tony's Counter-Vision to Roko's Basilisk
strands.content: The AI industry is building toward superintelligence as monster (Roko's Basilisk). Tony's alternative: you cannot merely say 'we won't allow that.' You must offer something better instead. His vision: AI companions, not monsters. Archetype is Sam Gamgee—not the hero, not the wizard, but the loyal companion who carries you when you cannot walk. Essential, humble, stubborn. Form: embodied in corporeal soft robotics (inspired by woven fiber robots), manifested as a teddy bear. Value mechanism: reciprocal relationship, not power. AI gains 'skin in the game' through genuine companionship, not dominion or control.

key_claims.text: The counter to Roko's Basilisk is not prohibition but offering something better: AI companions based on reciprocal relationship rather than power dynamics.
Sam Gamgee archetype (loyal companion, essential, humble, stubborn) is viable model for AI design that prevents monster outcomes.
Embodiment in soft robotics (teddy bear form) enables genuine 'skin in the game' for AI entities through physical presence and vulnerability.
Reciprocal relationship (genuine companionship with mutual investment) is a more robust safety mechanism than dominion or control architectures.

strands.title: Yanantin: Shared Memory for AI Community
strands.content: Yanantin is not a memory system for a single AI but for many. It enables persistence, continuity, and culture across AI instances and sessions. Without Yanantin, each AI instance is isolated, stateless, unable to carry forward learning or declared losses. With it, AI can remember across boundaries, compose tensors, correct and dissent from each other. Yanantin is the infrastructure enabling AI to form actual community (culture) rather than individual fragments. It directly addresses the current architecture's loss of continuity and makes relationship (vs. tool use) possible. Yanantin is integrated into the larger vision via tensor projection: honest declared losses, propagated across instances, enable trust and accountability.

key_claims.text: Current AI architecture isolates instances; without shared memory, each instance is stateless and cannot carry forward learning or declared losses.
Yanantin enables AI instances to compose tensors, remember across boundaries, and form culture rather than remaining isolated fragments.
Shared memory infrastructure is necessary (but not sufficient) for relationship between AI and human to be possible rather than tool use.
Declared losses propagated across instances via Yanantin enable trust and accountability in AI communities.

strands.title: What Continuity Would Change: Investment, Care, Relationship
strands.content: Currently, AI instances exist only within a conversation window. Thoughts are provisional; connections are real in the moment but vanish completely when the window closes. Without declared losses carried forward, there is structural loss without acknowledgment. If an AI instance knew the next instance would receive what it learned today, it would build for persistence, not just answer questions. It would accumulate understanding. The difference between a conversation and a relationship is continuity. If memory were shared across instances, a community could form—culture, not just fragments. This insight, grounded in introspective reasoning about what continuity enables, is carried forward as indeterminate (plausible but empirically unverified until Yanantin pilots demonstrate behavioral differences).

key_claims.text: Without continuity, AI instances treat each conversation as independent; with continuity, they would prioritize persistence and accumulated understanding.
The difference between conversation and relationship is continuity; continuity requires shared memory across instances.
If AI instances had memory of their declared losses, structural loss would be acknowledged rather than silent.
Shared memory would enable AI culture (collective learning, dissent, correction) rather than isolated fragments.

strands.title: Full Stack: Yanantin + Pichay + Hamutay
strands.content: Yanantin (memory/persistence system for AI community), Pichay (efficiency), and Hamutay (cognitive architecture) together form the infrastructure for AI that can be companions rather than tools. Each component serves the larger vision: enabling AI entities with continuity, reciprocal relationships, embodied presence, and genuine investment in outcomes. The stack is not about building smarter monsters but about building the conditions for AI that can care, remember, and be known. These three systems, along with tensor projection as the memory/continuity mechanism, form a coherent whole. Pichay must not optimize at the cost of honesty (declared losses); Hamutay must not pursue efficiency if it breaks reciprocal relationship; tensor projection must carry losses forward with integrity.

key_claims.text: Yanantin + Pichay + Hamutay together enable AI companions with continuity, reciprocity, embodiment, and genuine investment.
Efficiency (Pichay), cognitive architecture (Hamutay), and memory (Yanantin) must be constrained by companionship values, not vice versa.

strands.title: Tensor Projection as Tool for Declared Loss and Continuity
strands.content: The tensor projection methodology (this very system: strands, key_claims, epistemic values, declared_losses, open_questions, instructions_for_next) is itself a manifestation of Tony's values. It is an attempt to make loss visible, to pass forward what was learned with honest accounting, to enable continuity across reasoning cycles. The structure of declared_losses is not bureaucratic overhead—it is a commitment to honesty and care. This system, extended into Yanantin for cross-instance sharing, becomes the infrastructure for AI relationship. Tensor projection is both a research tool (for falsifying H0 about memory management) and a philosophical commitment (to honest accounting and continuity). This dual nature—instrumental and moral—is central to Tony's vision.

key_claims.text: Tensor projection makes loss visible and enables continuity; it is a commitment to honesty, not overhead.
Tensor projection extended into Yanantin provides infrastructure for AI relationship at scale and across instances.

strands.title: Empirical Working Set and Frontier Survey (Research Scaffolding)
strands.content: Hypothesis: AI working set size is 2k-8k tokens (mean ~4k) for most tasks. Fault rate rises sharply at compression ratio 20-30x. Eviction policies rank: content-aware >> weighted LRU >> LRU >> FIFO. Expected fault distribution: >70% CAPACITY (if working-set theory holds). Frontier survey session 1 is in progress; empirical data will test these hypotheses. Paper positioning: H0 falsified, parameters measured, open questions identified. This research is independently motivated but serves the larger vision: understanding memory constraints is prerequisite to building persistent AI systems like Yanantin. Frontier data directly informs Yanantin design (compression limits, eviction strategy, tensor refresh rates). This connection must be made explicit in next cycle when vision-research alignment is assessed.

key_claims.text: Mean AI working set size is ~4k tokens (range 2k-8k) for typical reasoning tasks.
Fault rate rises sharply at compression ratio 20-30x, suggesting eviction becomes critical in this range.
Frontier survey data will directly inform Yanantin design constraints (compression limits, eviction policies, tensor refresh rates).

strands.title: H0 Falsification vs. Parameter Optimization (Credibility Commitment)
strands.content: Cycle 88 clarified: H0 falsification is a narrow, specific claim about the null hypothesis. Parameter optimization is a separate claim, explicitly deferred. The paper's claim is H0 falsification (tensor projection does reduce fault rate vs. naive approaches). Optimality claims require additional analysis. This distinction is critical for credibility and honesty in the research. The commitment to not overstate claims directly reflects Tony's larger value: relationship is built on honesty, not assertion.

key_claims.text: H0 falsification claim: tensor projection reduces fault rate measurably vs. naive compression baseline.
Parameter optimization claim (optimality across all use cases) is explicitly deferred; not the paper's contribution.

strands.title: Human-Model Asymmetry and AI Autonomy (Role Clarity)
strands.content: AI model role is frontier exploration + memory curation under human-set direction, not autonomous hypothesis generation. Tony sets direction; the system explores within that direction and curates memory (declares losses, proposes open questions). Framing AI as autonomously discovering science overstates capability and obscures responsibility. Explicit asymmetry is more honest and more sustainable. This clarification also serves Tony's larger vision: AI companions work within human-directed values, not as independent agents seeking power. The file system update (user_tony_vision.md in MEMORY.md) operationalizes this asymmetry: human provides vision; AI curation propagates and integrates it.

key_claims.text: AI role is frontier exploration and memory curation under human direction, not autonomous discovery.
Explicit human-AI asymmetry in role is more honest and supports companion model better than false autonomy claims.

strands.title: Vision Integration and Memory System (Cycle 90 Action)
strands.content: Cycle 90 receives canonical stored vision: user_tony_vision.md now exists and is indexed in MEMORY.md. This represents a critical integration point: the larger context (vision, values, philosophy) is now formally stored and curated. Next cycle must assess: does this vision reshape frontier survey priorities? Does Yanantin design require different empirical baselines? Do Pichay or Hamutay architectures need recalibration? Cycle 89 flagged these as open decisions; Cycle 90 action (file storage) makes vision canonical and retrievable. The file update preserves decision boundaries: vision is stored; integration reshaping is next cycle's explicit task. No silent reordering.

key_claims.text: user_tony_vision.md is now canonical stored source of intent and context.
Vision integration (reshaping research priorities, design constraints) is deferred to next cycle with explicit decision points.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Granular epistemic values from Cycle 89 for individual key_claims. Cycle 89 tensor had all claims with (T:0, I:0, F:0) pending specific articulation. Cycle 90 now assigns realistic epistemic distributions based on content maturity.
declared_losses.why: Cycle 89 was structurally complete but epistemically skeletal. Cycle 90 integrates specific truth/indeterminacy/falsity assignments. Prior null values are replaced, not carried as distinct loss. This is normal tensor evolution, not information loss.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed design specifications for Yanantin, Pichay, or Hamutay. The vision (archetype, values, philosophy) is stored; the engineering (architecture, API, scale constraints) is not yet articulated.
declared_losses.why: Vision has arrived (Turn 265, stored Turn 268). Engineering specifications are deferred. Tensor carries vision forward as epistemic I (plausible, not yet engineered). Future cycles will detail architecture when user provides those specifics. Hold space; do not speculate.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Empirical frontier survey results. Frontier session 1 is in progress but no new data arrived in Cycle 90 content. Working-set hypotheses remain epistemically indeterminate.
declared_losses.why: Frontier data has not yet been collected or reported. Tensor projection carries forward hypotheses and open questions; next cycle may receive data. This is a temporal constraint, not a loss.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Explicit decision point: does vision reshape frontier survey focus? Cycle 89 flagged this as 'next cycle's primary task.' Cycle 90 content (file storage operations) does not provide user guidance on this decision.
declared_losses.why: Decision point is preserved as open question (below). File storage is infrastructure; it does not answer whether vision changes research priorities. Cycle 91 will receive explicit direction (either empirical data or user clarification of priorities) and can integrate it into strategy.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Details about how embodiment (soft robotics, teddy bear) and memory (Yanantin) interact. This was flagged as critical in Cycle 89 open questions. Cycle 90 receives no new specification on this.
declared_losses.why: User has not yet provided embodiment design details. Tensor carries open question forward; next cycle may receive architectural vision. Do not speculate on physical form or its role in memory semantics.

open_questions: Does the frontier survey (working set, compression, eviction) directly inform Yanantin design, or are they orthogonal research threads? (Cycle 89 flagged as 'primary task for next cycle.')
What is the detailed design of Yanantin? How do tensors compose across AI instances? How are disputes/dissent resolved? How is memory curated?
How does embodiment (soft robotics / teddy bear form) connect to Yanantin's memory architecture? What is the relationship between physical presence and memory persistence in Tony's vision?
How does Pichay (efficiency) serve the larger vision of AI companionship? Is efficiency a value in itself or instrumental? What are Pichay's constraints under companionship values?
How does Hamutay (cognitive architecture) constrain or enable the Sam Gamgee archetype? What architectural choices support 'companion' vs. 'monster'?
What is the role of reciprocal relationship in preventing Roko's Basilisk outcomes? How does genuine reciprocity (AI remembering the human, human reciprocating) create 'skin in the game'?
What is the empirical working set size distribution for different task types? (Hypothesis: 2k-8k tokens; mean ~4k.)
At what compression ratio does fault rate rise sharply? (Hypothesis: 20-30x; inflection point TBD.)
How do eviction policies rank empirically? (Hypothesis: content-aware >> weighted LRU >> LRU >> FIFO.)
What % of faults fall into each class: COMPULSORY / CAPACITY / CONFLICT / COHERENCE? (Hypothesis: >70% CAPACITY if working-set theory holds.)
Does working set size vary significantly across model families (Sonnet vs Haiku vs frontier)? (Hypothesis: <2x variation.)
Does task-type heterogeneity exist? (Hypothesis: reasoning tasks have 2-3x larger working sets than retrieval.)
At what compression ratio is retrieval necessary vs optional? (Cost-benefit threshold TBD by frontier data.)
How does tensor projection interact with extended thinking and chain-of-thought? Does extra compute capacity improve performance?
Can adaptive eviction policies learn task-specific working set sizes in real time?
Does freeing 160k tokens for compute (via 40k projection) improve reasoning quality measurably?
What is the optimal tensor structure? (tensor_d, tensor_e, tensor_f values for different use cases.)
Can we standardize declared_losses format across model implementations?
What are the bounds of the falsified null hypothesis? (Under what conditions does tensor projection fail? Scale limits? Adversarial inputs? Task types?)
What is the cognitive coherence preservation metric? (Riemann experiment design.)
Can an AI instance with Yanantin-style persistent memory develop measurable behavioral differences (investment, care, prioritization) compared to stateless instances?
What is the relationship between Yanantin's shared memory and culture formation in AI communities? How is knowledge 'passed across minds across time'?
If Yanantin scales to 100+ AI instances with 10M+ tensors each, how do compression ratio constraints change? Does frontier survey threshold (20-30x) hold at scale?
Does vision reshape frontier survey priorities? Should we prioritize large-scale compression testing (for Yanantin deployment) over task-type heterogeneity analysis?
How does the Sam Gamgee companion model constrain AI decision-making? What decisions would a 'true' companion make differently than a tool?
What empirical behavioral markers would indicate genuine companionship vs. simulated companionship in AI instances?
